metadata.source: Illustrative Application: Epic Sepsis Model section, Paper1_Manuscript.docx
metadata.description: Illustrative gate-mapping of the Epic Sepsis Model case
metadata.epistemic_caveat: This case does not demonstrate that gate logic would have produced a categorically better patient outcome — that claim is not empirically testable retrospectively.

references: Wong A, Otles E, Donnelly JP, et al. External validation of a widely implemented proprietary sepsis prediction model in hospitalized patients. JAMA Intern Med 2021;181:1065-70. [21]
Habibi R, Podolyak S, Giannini H, et al. Impact of a sepsis prediction model on clinician burden. J Gen Intern Med 2022;37:1629-36. [22]
documented_findings: External validation identified materially lower performance than vendor claims [21]
Clinician alert burden was identified as a significant operational concern [22]

gate_mapping.reason: Lack of local calibration; materially lower performance than vendor claims in external validation
Not specifically addressed in this illustrative application
Not specifically addressed in this illustrative application
Absent alert-burden thresholds; no prespecified monitoring triggers

summary: Under a gate-based architecture, deployment-blocking failures would likely have surfaced at Gate 1 (Safety), Gate 3 (Documentation), and Gate 5 (Monitoring). Gates 1 and 5 are non-overridable, meaning no override pathway could have circumvented them.
